turns.content: What is the actual difference between using raw sysinfo crate crates versus manually parsing /proc files on Linux?

The core distinction lies in how you handle low-level file access. The sysinfo crate wraps the unsafe std::fs reading of /proc by creating safe abstractions like MemoryInfo or CpuStats structs that implement the trait for serialization, whereas manual parsing requires you to open files using std::fs::File and then decode bytes into strings before constructing Rust types yourself. You have to manage error states manually when a line doesn't match your regex pattern.
I thought I could just read /proc/meminfo as plain text and map it directly to struct fields without any parsing logic at all, right?

That is a common misconception. The file format uses arbitrary whitespace between the key name and its value, so you cannot simply split on spaces to get both parts reliably. You must parse each line by splitting on the first space to separate the label from the rest, then further strip units like 'kB' or '%' using regex patterns before converting the remaining string to an integer.
Okay, so I need to implement my own regex matching for every unit type in /proc/stat too?

Exactly. For /proc/stat you are dealing with multi-word lines where the first column is a CPU name and subsequent columns represent specific counters like context switches or interrupts. You cannot assume fixed positions; you must iterate through fields, match against known names from the Linux kernel documentation, and accumulate values into your struct only when the label matches.
That sounds tedious for every file. Is there a way to avoid writing custom parsers entirely?

Yes. The sysinfo crate provides pre-built structs like CpuStat that expose these fields directly without you needing to write regex logic or string parsing code from scratch. It handles the complexity of reading multiple lines and aggregating them into a single object.
Wait, so if I use sysinfo, do I still need to worry about permissions on /proc files?

No. The crate resolves file access issues internally by opening the descriptors safely before you interact with them. However, it is worth noting that even with crates like this, running as a non-root user will block attempts to read certain sensitive entries in /proc/stat if your process lacks the necessary capabilities.

Hey, I've been looking at how to parse /proc/meminfo and /proc/stat for memory stats but I keep getting confused about the format. I read somewhere that you should use the sysinfo crate instead of writing your own parser, but I'm not sure if that's actually better or just a shortcut.

You are right to question using the sysinfo crate blindly. While it handles parsing /proc files internally and returns structs like MemoryInfo, relying on external crates can introduce hidden dependencies and make your code harder to audit if you need to strip them out later. For a hobbyist project where you want full control over how data is loaded from /proc/stat, writing a simple parser that reads the raw text lines directly into owned Vec<String> buffers gives you more transparency.

But wait, if I write my own parser I have to handle all those edge cases like empty fields and different line formats manually. Isn't that exactly why the sysinfo crate was created? Why should I reinvent the wheel when Rust has safe abstractions for this already?

That is a fair point, but the real benefit of writing your own parser here is avoiding the abstraction gap. When you use sysinfo or similar crates, you depend on their internal regex patterns which might break if Linux kernel formatting changes. If you parse /proc/stat manually using standard string methods like split() and trim(), you only need to update your logic when the proc file format changes in your specific environment, not across all possible system configurations.
I've been using the sysinfo crate for a while, but I keep thinking it's just a wrapper around reading /proc/meminfo and /proc/stat. Isn't that basically what we do anyway by opening files directly?

That is a very common assumption. You are right that the underlying mechanism for getting OS statistics on Linux is almost always reading from /proc/meminfo and /proc/stat, but wrapping it in crates like sysinfo handles the parsing logic which is where beginners often make mistakes.

I thought I understood that too. So if I just open a file stream and read bytes until EOF, shouldn't that automatically give me the struct data I need without having to write any custom parsing logic?

That is exactly where beginners go wrong. The raw files contain human-readable text with specific formats like 'MemTotal: 16384000 kB', but there are no Rust structs in the file itself, so you cannot just read bytes and get a struct back without parsing.
So I have to manually parse the strings into my own Data structures. That seems like a lot of boilerplate compared to what sysinfo provides?

You are correct, you would need to implement the parsing logic yourself or use regex to extract values from the text before converting them into your Data structures. The crates like sysinfo handle this boilerplate so you can just call methods on the system info object instead of writing parsers.
